title: What is a Mutual Fund? Complete Guide for Indians 2026
meta_description: Complete mutual fund guide: types explained, SIP vs lumpsum, direct vs regular plans, best AMCs, taxation, how to start with ₹100, and 25+ FAQs answered.

hero.one_liner: A complete guide to mutual funds for Indian beginners — learn what they are, which types suit your goals, how to start a SIP with just ₹100, and avoid common mistakes that cost new investors lakhs.
hero.updated_date: Last reviewed: April 2026 • Sources: SEBI, AMFI, Income Tax Dept

sections.heading: What is a Mutual Fund?
content: A mutual fund is a professionally managed investment vehicle that pools money from thousands of investors and invests it in a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or other securities. Instead of picking individual stocks yourself, you hand over your money to an expert fund manager who makes investment decisions on behalf of all investors.
In return, you get 'units' of the fund — similar to owning shares in a company, except these units represent your share in the entire fund's portfolio.

Mutual funds in India are regulated by SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) and must follow strict rules about transparency, diversification, and investor protection. All fund performance, holdings, and fees are publicly disclosed every month.
As of 2026, India has 44 registered Asset Management Companies (AMCs) offering over 1,500 mutual fund schemes, managing assets worth over ₹65 lakh crore — a ten-fold growth from 2015.

The key advantage is professional management + diversification at low cost. When you invest ₹5,000 in an equity mutual fund, your money is spread across 40-60 stocks picked by an experienced fund manager.

Trying to replicate this on your own would need lakhs of rupees and full-time research. For beginners especially, mutual funds offer the simplest way to benefit from stock market growth without needing to pick individual stocks.

sections.heading: The Core Idea
sections.content: A mutual fund lets you own a piece of 40-60 different stocks with just ₹500. The fund manager does the research and buying; you just invest monthly and let compounding work.

sections.heading: How Mutual Funds Actually Work
content: Step 1: Pooling — Thousands of investors (retail and institutional) put money into a scheme. Each investor gets mutual fund 'units' proportional to their investment.

Step 2: Fund Manager invests — The fund manager, following a pre-defined investment objective, buys stocks/bonds/other securities. An equity fund buys stocks; a debt fund buys bonds; a hybrid fund buys both.
Step 3: NAV calculated daily — Net Asset Value (NAV) = Total value of fund assets ÷ Total units issued. If the fund holds ₹100 crore of assets and has issued 10 crore units, NAV = ₹10/unit.
NAV changes daily based on how the underlying stocks/bonds perform.
Step 4: You buy/sell at NAV — When you invest ₹10,000 and NAV is ₹10, you get 1,000 units. When you redeem, you receive the units × current NAV.
If NAV has grown to ₹12, you get ₹12,000 (20% return).
Step 5: Expenses deducted — The AMC charges an annual 'expense ratio' (0.1% to 2.5%) for managing the fund. This is automatically deducted from the fund's NAV daily, so you never pay it separately.

sections.caption: The complete journey of your money — from investor to securities to wealth
sections.description: This is how ₹5,000/month from lakhs of investors becomes a diversified portfolio managed by experts. You don't need to pick stocks yourself.

sections.heading: Mutual Funds vs Other Investments — Quick Comparison
Types of Mutual Funds in India
content: SEBI classifies mutual funds into several categories based on where they invest. Understanding these is critical to picking the right fund for your goals:
1. Equity Mutual Funds — Invest 65%+ in stocks.

Best for long-term goals (5+ years). Subtypes include: Large Cap (top 100 companies, safer), Mid Cap (101-250, growth), Small Cap (below 250, high risk+return), Multi Cap (mix of all), Flexi Cap (fund manager decides), Sectoral (only one sector like pharma/IT), ELSS (tax-saving with 3-year lock-in).
2. Debt Mutual Funds — Invest in bonds, government securities, corporate debt.
Lower risk than equity. Good for 1-3 year goals.
Subtypes: Liquid Funds (super short term, 0-91 days), Overnight Funds (1-day papers), Short Duration (1-3 years), Corporate Bond, Gilt Funds (only govt bonds). Returns typically 6-9% annually.
3. Hybrid Mutual Funds — Mix of equity and debt.

Balanced risk. Subtypes: Aggressive Hybrid (65-80% equity), Balanced Hybrid (40-60% equity), Conservative Hybrid (10-25% equity), Multi-Asset (equity + debt + gold), Arbitrage Funds (hedged equity, tax-efficient).
4. Index Funds & ETFs — Passively track an index like Nifty 50 or Sensex.
No fund manager stock-picking. Lowest expense ratio (0.1-0.3%).
Returns match the index. Best for beginners.
Popular: UTI Nifty 50, HDFC Sensex, Nippon India Nifty 50 ETF.
5. Solution-Oriented Funds — Retirement Funds (5-year lock-in or till retirement), Children's Funds (5-year lock-in or till child turns 18).
Useful for goal-linked saving.
6. International Funds — Invest in US/global stocks (Nasdaq 100, S&P 500 ETFs). Adds currency diversification but complex taxation.

sections.heading: SIP vs Lump Sum — Which is Better?
content: SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) is investing a fixed amount every month automatically. For example, ₹5,000 on the 5th of every month into a Nifty 50 index fund.
The amount stays fixed; units bought vary with NAV. When markets are high, you buy fewer units; when markets are low, you buy more.
This averages out your buying price — called Rupee Cost Averaging.
Lump Sum is investing a big amount at once. For example, putting ₹5 lakh into an equity fund on a single day.
Risky if markets crash right after, rewarding if markets rally.

Historical data from AMFI shows that for long-term goals (10+ years), both SIP and Lump Sum give similar final returns — because markets eventually grow. But SIP wins on psychology: you don't need to time the market, don't panic during crashes (you buy more cheap units), and it builds discipline.

The practical rule: If you have a bulk amount (bonus, inheritance, FD maturity), split it over 12 months via STP (Systematic Transfer Plan) instead of lump-summing. If you earn a salary and want to invest monthly, SIP is perfect.
Most first-time investors should start with SIP.

SIP math example: A ₹10,000/month SIP in a Nifty 50 index fund over 15 years (at 12% CAGR) grows to ₹50 lakh (you invested ₹18 lakh, gained ₹32 lakh). Increase SIP by 10% annually (called Step-up SIP) and the same grows to ₹72 lakh.

sections.text: ₹10,000/month SIP in a Nifty 50 index fund over 20 years becomes ₹1 Crore. You invested ₹24 lakh. Compounding added ₹76 lakh.

sections.description: You invested ₹24 lakh across 240 SIP installments. Compounding added ₹76 lakh on top. This is why starting early matters more than how much you invest — time does the heavy lifting.

sections.heading: Direct vs Regular Plans — The 1% Difference That Matters
content: Every mutual fund has two versions: Direct Plan and Regular Plan. They invest in the exact same portfolio but have different costs:
Direct Plan — You buy directly from the AMC or through apps like Groww, Kuvera, Coin (Zerodha). No distributor/agent is involved.
Expense ratio is 0.5-1% lower. No commission deducted.
Regular Plan — You buy through a bank, advisor, or distributor. They receive a trail commission (0.5-1% annually) as long as you stay invested.
This commission is deducted from your returns silently via a higher expense ratio.
The impact over 20 years: A ₹10,000/month SIP earning 12% in a Direct plan grows to ₹1.00 crore. The same SIP in a Regular plan with 1% higher expense ratio grows to only ₹85 lakh.
That's ₹15 lakh lost to distributor commissions. For 30 years, the gap widens to ₹60+ lakh.
Recommendation: Always choose Direct Plans. Use apps like Groww, Kuvera, Coin, ET Money for zero-commission investing.
If you need advisory help, pay a SEBI-registered fee-only Investment Advisor (RIA) a one-time or annual fee instead of paying through hidden commissions.

sections.heading: The ₹15 Lakh Decision
sections.content: Choosing Direct plans over Regular plans saves you ₹15+ lakh over 20 years on a ₹10K/month SIP. Use apps like Groww, Kuvera, or Coin — never buy mutual funds through a bank (they push Regular plans with hidden commissions).

sections.caption: Same fund, same manager, same returns — but 1% higher fee costs you ₹15 lakh
sections.description: This is why choosing Direct plans via Groww, Kuvera, or Coin (instead of buying through a bank or distributor) is the single most important decision for long-term mutual fund investors.

sections.heading: Top Mutual Fund AMCs in India — 2026
Taxation on Mutual Funds — 2026 Rules
content: Mutual fund taxation changed significantly in the Union Budget 2024. Here's the current structure as of FY 2025-26:
Equity Mutual Funds (65%+ in Indian stocks) — Short-Term Capital Gains (sold within 12 months): 20% flat. Long-Term Capital Gains (held over 12 months): 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25 lakh/year.
First ₹1.25 lakh of LTCG per financial year is tax-free.
Debt Mutual Funds — All gains (irrespective of holding period) taxed at your income tax slab rate. Before April 2023, debt funds had indexation benefit; now they don't.
Very tax-inefficient for high earners.
Hybrid Funds (if 65%+ equity) — Same as equity funds (STCG 20%, LTCG 12.5%). If less than 65% equity, treated as debt funds.
Check the fund's equity allocation carefully.
Arbitrage Funds — Technically equity (due to hedged equity exposure), so taxed like equity. A popular way for high earners to park money for 6-12 months with tax efficiency.
International Funds — Taxed as debt funds (your slab rate), regardless of underlying assets. This reduces their appeal.
ELSS (Tax-Saving Equity Funds) — Investments up to ₹1.5 lakh/year qualify for Section 80C deduction (old tax regime only). 3-year lock-in per SIP installment. Gains taxed as equity.

sections.heading: How to Start Investing in Mutual Funds — Step by Step

steps.title: Complete your KYC
steps.description: One-time process. Submit PAN, Aadhaar, bank details, and selfie on any AMC website (SBI MF, HDFC MF) or aggregator app (Groww, Kuvera, Coin). KYC is valid across all mutual funds — do it once, invest anywhere. Takes 10-15 minutes for e-KYC, or 3-7 days for traditional KYC.

steps.title: Download a direct plan app
steps.description: Use Groww, Kuvera, Coin (by Zerodha), ET Money, or the AMC's own app. These give access to Direct Plans (no distributor commission). Avoid buying mutual funds through your bank — they'll push Regular Plans with hidden commissions.

steps.title: Choose your fund type based on goal
steps.description: Retirement (20+ years): Flexi Cap or Nifty 50 Index Fund. Child's education (10-15 years): Large Cap or Index Fund + Aggressive Hybrid. Home down payment (3-5 years): Balanced Advantage Fund or Aggressive Hybrid. Emergency fund: Liquid Fund. Avoid sectoral funds as a beginner.

steps.title: Pick specific funds using ratings
steps.description: Check 5-year and 10-year returns on Value Research or Morningstar. Look for funds with: consistent returns (not just one good year), expense ratio under 1%, 4+ star rating, same fund manager for 3+ years. For beginners, a simple Nifty 50 Index Fund (UTI, HDFC, SBI) beats 70% of active funds over 10 years.

steps.title: Set up your first SIP
steps.description: Start with ₹1,000-5,000 monthly. Choose date between 1st-5th (right after salary credit). Enable 'auto-debit' mandate via UPI or net banking. SIP runs indefinitely until you stop it — no renewal needed. Never stop SIP during market crashes; that's when you buy the most units.

steps.title: Diversify across 3-4 funds maximum
steps.description: Too many funds defeats the purpose. Ideal beginner portfolio: 1 Nifty 50 Index Fund (50% allocation), 1 Mid Cap Fund (25%), 1 Flexi Cap Fund (25%). Review annually but don't switch frequently — let funds work over 5+ years to see real growth.

steps.title: Review annually, not monthly
steps.description: Mutual funds need TIME to perform. Check portfolio once a year. Rebalance only if allocations drift by more than 10%. Don't exit a fund just because it underperformed for 6 months — that's normal. Exit only if fund manager changes, expense ratio increases, or 3+ year underperformance vs category.

steps.title: Increase SIP with income growth
steps.description: Set up 'Step-up SIP' to increase your SIP by 10% every year automatically. ₹5,000 starting SIP increased 10% yearly over 20 years at 12% returns grows to ₹50 lakh. Same ₹5,000 without step-up grows to only ₹30 lakh. This single habit changes your wealth trajectory dramatically.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes Beginners Make
rows: 1% higher expense = ₹15-60 lakh lost over 20-30 years
You stop buying when units are cheapest
Continue or increase SIP during crashes
Winners rotate; top 2024 fund often lags in 2025
Betting on one sector = concentration risk
Converts paper loss to real loss
1% extra fee = 20% less wealth long term

sections.heading: The #1 Beginner Myth
sections.content: Don't pick a fund based on low NAV. A fund with NAV ₹10 and a fund with NAV ₹100 give identical returns for the same money. NAV is price per unit — it's meaningless. Always compare 5-year returns and expense ratio instead.

sections.heading: How to Track and Manage Your Mutual Fund Portfolio
content: CAMS Online / KFinTech — These are the two Registrar and Transfer Agents (RTAs) that maintain records for all Indian mutual funds.
Register at camsonline.com and kfintech.com to get a consolidated view of ALL your mutual fund holdings across AMCs.
Free to use.
CAS (Consolidated Account Statement) — Monthly statement showing all your mutual fund + demat holdings in one document. Sent automatically by email from CAMS/KFinTech.
Or download anytime from their portals.
MFU (Mutual Fund Utility) — Single platform to buy, sell, switch across multiple AMCs. Useful if you hold funds across 5+ AMCs.
Free to register at mfuindia.com.
Track via apps — Your chosen platform (Groww/Kuvera/Coin) shows real-time portfolio value, XIRR returns, asset allocation. Check monthly at most; daily checking triggers unnecessary trading.
Nomination is critical — Always add a nominee to your mutual fund folio. Without a nominee, your family will need to go through cumbersome legal procedures to claim your investments after you.
Update nominee whenever family situations change.

sections.heading: Official Sources & Further Reading
rows: AMFI — Association of Mutual Funds in India
Industry data, AUM, fund NAVs, KYC status, all AMC info
Investor protection, disclosure rules, categorization norms
Free fund ratings, comparisons, portfolio analysis
Free consolidated account statement across AMCs
Alternative CAS service for non-CAMS AMCs
File ITR, check STCG/LTCG rates on mutual fund gains

sections.heading: Editorial Note
content: This guide was written by the KnowledgeKendra editorial team and last fact-checked in April 2026. All tax rates, regulations, and fund data are current as of the last review date.

Mutual fund rules change frequently — SEBI updates categorization norms, Union Budget changes tax treatment, and AMCs launch/merge schemes. We commit to updating this page within 7 days of any major regulatory change.
This content is for educational purposes only and is not personalized investment advice. Mutual funds are subject to market risks; read all scheme-related documents carefully before investing.
Past performance of any fund does not guarantee future returns. Before investing a significant amount, consider consulting a SEBI-registered Investment Advisor (RIA).
Disclosure: KnowledgeKendra has no commercial relationship with any AMC, distributor, or fund mentioned. AMC rankings are based on publicly available AUM data from AMFI as of March 2026.
Fund names are mentioned for educational purposes only and should not be interpreted as recommendations.

faqs.question: Can I lose all my money in mutual funds?
faqs.answer: Extremely unlikely but theoretically possible with sectoral/thematic funds. Diversified equity mutual funds have never gone to zero — even during 2008 crash, Nifty 50 dropped 60% then recovered. For diversified funds, your worst-case loss over a 7+ year horizon is historically limited to 20-30% temporarily. Your money is held at SEBI-registered custodians, separate from AMC's own funds, so even if the AMC goes bankrupt, your money is safe.

faqs.question: What is the minimum amount to start mutual funds?
faqs.answer: ₹100/month for SIP in most funds. Some funds accept ₹500 minimum lump sum. You don't need lakhs to start. ₹500/month over 20 years at 12% CAGR becomes ₹5 lakh. ₹5,000/month over 20 years becomes ₹50 lakh. Starting small and increasing with income growth is better than waiting to accumulate 'enough' to invest.

faqs.question: Mutual fund vs FD — which is better?
faqs.answer: Depends on goal and timeframe. For goals under 2 years: FD wins (guaranteed returns, zero risk). For goals 3-5 years: Hybrid funds or Balanced Advantage funds beat FD with moderate risk. For goals 5+ years: Equity mutual funds historically outperform FDs by 4-6% annually. Over 20 years, ₹10,000/month in FD (7%) grows to ₹52 lakh; same in equity mutual fund (12%) grows to ₹1 crore. Tax efficiency also favors equity funds (12.5% LTCG vs slab rate on FD interest).

faqs.question: What is Direct vs Regular plan?
faqs.answer: Direct plans have 0.5-1% lower expense ratio because no distributor commission is paid. Same portfolio, same fund manager, just cheaper. Over 20 years, this 1% difference costs you ₹15+ lakh on a ₹10,000/month SIP. Always choose Direct plans via apps like Groww, Kuvera, Coin. If you need advice, pay a SEBI-registered RIA a fixed fee instead of hidden distributor commissions.

faqs.question: Do I need a demat account for mutual funds?
faqs.answer: No, demat is optional for mutual funds. You can invest directly through AMC websites or platforms like Groww, Kuvera, Coin without a demat. Demat is only needed if you want to hold mutual fund units in demat form (required for some ETFs and closed-ended funds). For regular SIP in open-ended funds, demat is completely unnecessary.

faqs.question: What is NAV and how is it calculated?
faqs.answer: NAV (Net Asset Value) is the price of one unit of a mutual fund. Calculated daily as: (Total Assets - Expenses) ÷ Total Units Outstanding. If a fund has ₹1,000 crore in assets with 10 crore units outstanding, NAV = ₹100. You buy units at end-of-day NAV, not intraday price. Unlike stock prices, NAV doesn't fluctuate minute-to-minute — it's set once per business day around 10 PM.

faqs.question: Should I buy a fund with low NAV?
faqs.answer: This is the #1 beginner misconception. NAV is NOT like share price. A ₹10 NAV fund and ₹100 NAV fund give the SAME returns for the same money invested. Example: invest ₹10,000 in Fund A (NAV ₹10, get 1000 units) and Fund B (NAV ₹100, get 100 units). Both funds grow 10%: Fund A becomes ₹11,000 (NAV ₹11), Fund B becomes ₹11,000 (NAV ₹110). Same return. Never pick a fund based on low NAV — compare 5-year returns, expense ratio, and consistency.

faqs.question: What is SIP and why is it popular?
faqs.answer: SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) is investing a fixed amount monthly into a mutual fund automatically. ₹5,000 SIP debits from your bank on a fixed date and buys fund units. SIP is popular because: (1) Rupee Cost Averaging — you buy more units when markets fall, fewer when high. (2) Discipline — automates investing. (3) Affordability — start with ₹100. (4) Psychology — no market timing needed. Over 20 years, SIP investors outperform lump-sum investors 70% of the time.

faqs.question: What happens if I stop SIP?
faqs.answer: Nothing bad. Your existing units continue to grow. Just no new investments happen. You can restart anytime. However, stopping SIP during market crashes is the worst timing — that's when you're buying the most units per rupee invested. Smart investors increase SIP during crashes; beginners often panic-stop.

faqs.question: Can I withdraw mutual fund money anytime?
faqs.answer: Yes for most funds (called 'open-ended'). Redeem via your app; money credits in T+1 to T+3 days depending on fund type. Liquid funds credit same day if redeemed before 1 PM. Exceptions: ELSS funds have 3-year lock-in per SIP installment. Solution-oriented funds (retirement/child) have 5-year lock-in. Don't withdraw equity funds before 3-5 years unless absolutely needed — you'll miss compounding.

faqs.question: Are mutual funds safe?
faqs.answer: Yes, mutual funds are heavily regulated by SEBI. Your money is held at independent custodian banks, separate from the AMC's accounts. Even if an AMC collapses, your money is safe — transferred to another AMC. However, mutual funds carry MARKET RISK — NAV can fluctuate daily. Equity fund NAV can drop 20-40% during crashes. This isn't theft or fraud; it's normal market behavior. Safe from fraud, risky from markets — know the difference.

faqs.question: What is an expense ratio?
faqs.answer: The annual fee charged by the AMC for managing the fund. Expressed as a percentage of your investment. Passive index funds: 0.1-0.3%. Active equity funds: 1.0-2.5%. Deducted daily from NAV — you never pay separately. A 1% higher expense ratio reduces your wealth by 20% over 30 years. Always prefer funds with expense ratio under 1% for active funds and under 0.5% for index funds.

faqs.question: What is ELSS and how does it save tax?
faqs.answer: ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme) is a tax-saving equity mutual fund. Investments up to ₹1.5 lakh/year qualify for Section 80C deduction under OLD tax regime. Under new tax regime (default from FY 2024-25), ELSS doesn't save tax. Has 3-year lock-in per SIP installment. Returns are fully equity-based — historically 12-15% annually. Popular ELSS funds: Quant ELSS, Mirae Asset ELSS, Parag Parikh ELSS.

faqs.question: Can NRIs invest in Indian mutual funds?
faqs.answer: Yes, NRIs can invest in most Indian mutual funds through NRE or NRO bank accounts. Required: PAN, NRE/NRO account, FATCA declaration. Restrictions apply for US/Canada NRIs — many AMCs don't accept them due to compliance costs. AMCs that accept US/Canada NRIs: ICICI Prudential, SBI, UTI, L&T. Taxation differs: 20% TDS on STCG and 12.5% TDS on LTCG for NRIs (vs lower for residents).

faqs.question: How many mutual funds should I own?
faqs.answer: 3 to 5 for most beginners. Adding more creates duplication with no extra benefit. Ideal portfolio: 1 Nifty 50 Index Fund (50% allocation), 1 Mid Cap or Flexi Cap Fund (25%), 1 International or Small Cap (15-25%), optionally 1 Debt/Hybrid Fund for stability (remaining). Adding 10+ funds gives you a 'fund collection' not a portfolio — most advanced investors stick to 3-5 core holdings.

faqs.question: What is XIRR in mutual funds?
faqs.answer: XIRR (Extended Internal Rate of Return) is the annualized return on SIP investments, accounting for different cash flow dates. Since SIP invests different amounts on different dates, simple CAGR doesn't work. XIRR gives the true annual return. Most apps show it automatically. If your SIP shows 15% XIRR, your money is growing 15% annually when accounting for when each installment was invested.

faqs.question: Is it okay to invest in mutual funds without professional advice?
faqs.answer: For beginners investing through SIPs in diversified index funds, self-investing is fine. Most beginners overthink fund selection. Just pick a Nifty 50 Index Fund from a large AMC (UTI, HDFC, SBI, ICICI) via a Direct plan on Groww/Kuvera — you'll beat 70% of advisors over 10 years. Professional advice becomes useful when: you have ₹50 lakh+ to invest, complex tax situation, goal-based planning needed, or multiple income sources.

faqs.question: What happens to my SIP if the fund manager changes?
faqs.answer: Usually no immediate impact — the investment philosophy continues for 12-18 months. New fund manager may gradually reshape portfolio. Watch closely for 6-12 months after change. If: returns significantly lag category for 12 months OR new manager announces major strategy shift, consider switching to another fund. Don't panic-exit immediately; give new manager reasonable time.

faqs.question: Can I invest in US stocks through Indian mutual funds?
faqs.answer: Yes, via International Funds. Options: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund, Franklin US Opportunities Fund, Edelweiss US Technology Fund. Adds currency diversification (rupee depreciation against dollar boosts returns). Warning: taxed as debt funds (slab rate) not equity, so tax-inefficient. Limit to 10-15% of portfolio for diversification only.

faqs.question: What is SWP in mutual funds?
faqs.answer: SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan) is the opposite of SIP — withdraw a fixed amount monthly from your fund. Used post-retirement for regular income. Example: Invest ₹50 lakh in Balanced Advantage Fund, set up ₹40,000 monthly SWP. You get regular income; remaining corpus continues to grow. Tax-efficient because only the gain portion (not principal) is taxed.

faqs.question: Should I redeem mutual funds during market crashes?
faqs.answer: No, never. Redeeming during crashes converts 'paper loss' into 'real loss.' Markets always recover — 2008 crash, 2020 COVID crash, 2022 correction, all recovered within 12-24 months. If you needed the money, you shouldn't have invested in equity. Equity mutual funds require 5-7+ year mindset. Instead of redeeming during crashes, INCREASE your SIP — you're buying cheap.

faqs.question: How are mutual funds taxed in new vs old tax regime?
faqs.answer: Capital gains taxation is the SAME in both regimes: STCG 20%, LTCG 12.5% (on gains above ₹1.25 lakh/year) for equity funds. The difference is only for tax-saving investments like ELSS: Old regime allows ₹1.5 lakh Section 80C deduction via ELSS; new regime does NOT. If you're in new regime (now default), ELSS loses its tax advantage — still okay as a pure equity fund, but no special benefit.

faqs.question: Where can I track all my mutual funds in one place?
faqs.answer: Use CAMS and KFinTech — India's two RTAs (Registrar and Transfer Agents) cover all 44 AMCs. Register once on both, get monthly Consolidated Account Statement (CAS) by email. Alternatively, apps like Groww and Kuvera auto-import all your holdings across AMCs and show unified portfolio.

faqs.question: What's the difference between AMC and fund manager?
faqs.answer: AMC (Asset Management Company) is the company that runs the mutual fund business — HDFC AMC, SBI Funds Management, etc. The Fund Manager is an individual employed by the AMC who makes daily investment decisions for a specific fund. One AMC has many fund managers; one fund manager may run multiple schemes. Both matter: AMC for processes/safety, fund manager for performance.

faqs.question: Can students and minors invest in mutual funds?
faqs.answer: Yes. Students (18+) with PAN can invest directly. Minors (under 18) can have folios opened by a guardian (parent) — minor's PAN and birth proof required. On turning 18, folio is converted with fresh KYC. Students should start small (₹500-1000/month) in a Nifty 50 Index Fund to learn market behavior. The 10+ year compounding advantage for young investors is unbeatable.
